learn.title: The Duck Curve Problem
learn.intro: Solar's midday peak creates a 'duck curve' in electricity demand, challenging grid stability at sunset. Managing this dip requires innovative solutions to maintain grid reliability.
learn.c1_text: Solar panels generate maximum power at noon, causing a sharp drop in demand. This creates a 'duck curve' where evening demand spikes require rapid ramping of other power sources.
learn.c2_text: As solar output drops after sunset, grid operators must quickly activate gas plants or storage to meet demand. This rapid response can strain infrastructure and increase costs.
learn.c3_text: India's 70 GW solar capacity (2024) exacerbates the duck curve. The grid must manage 20 GW+ solar curtailment during peak hours to maintain stability.
learn.stat2: Duck curve causes 15% of grid instability incidents
learn.stat3: Gas plants ramp up 40% faster during evening peaks

learn.title: Bifacial Solar Panels
learn.intro: Bifacial panels capture sunlight from both sides, boosting energy yield by 10-25%. This innovation is transforming solar farm efficiency and land use.
learn.c1_text: Bifacial panels use reflective surfaces to capture light from both front and back, increasing energy output by up to 25% compared to traditional panels.
learn.c2_text: Albedo (surface reflectivity) determines backside gain. Concrete (0.12) vs. white gravel (0.35) can double rear-side generation.
learn.c3_text: Indian bifacial farms in Gujarat achieve 22% higher output. PM Surya Ghar Yojana promotes bifacial adoption for 10% efficiency gains.
learn.stat2: Albedo of 0.3 boosts rear-side generation by 30%
learn.stat3: India's bifacial farms generate 1.2 GW extra annually
learn.stat4: Bifacial adoption reduces land use by 15%

learn.title: Building Integrated Photovoltaics
learn.intro: BIPV turns buildings into power plants, reducing urban energy demand and transforming architecture into sustainable infrastructure.
learn.c1_text: BIPV integrates solar into roofs, facades, and windows. A 100 m² BIPV roof can generate 15-20 kWh/day, powering an average home.
learn.c2_text: Transparent solar glass (18% efficiency) allows light while generating power. India's first BIPV skyscraper in Mumbai generates 300 kW/year.
learn.c3_text: India's 80 million rooftops could host 500 GW BIPV. Smart Cities Mission allocates 20% of funding for BIPV integration in new developments.
learn.stat3: India's BIPV market to reach $2B by 2030
learn.stat4: BIPV reduces urban cooling costs by 10%

learn.title: Carbon Markets & Credits
learn.intro: Carbon markets create financial incentives for clean energy, enabling India to monetize its solar potential while meeting climate targets.
learn.c1_text: Carbon credits let companies offset emissions by purchasing verified reductions. India's carbon credit trading could unlock $10B annually by 2030.
learn.c2_text: Cap-and-trade systems set emission limits. India's 2022 pilot in Tamil Nadu reduced industrial emissions by 12% through credit trading.
learn.c3_text: India's carbon credit policy (2023) allows solar projects to earn ₹800/ton. The first trading platform launched in Gujarat in 2024.
learn.stat1: India's carbon credit trading could unlock $10B annually
learn.stat2: Tamil Nadu pilot reduced emissions by 12%
learn.stat3: Solar projects earn ₹800/ton in carbon credits
learn.stat4: Gujarat's first carbon trading platform launched in 2024

learn.title: Lithium-Ion Battery Storage
learn.intro: Lithium-ion batteries store solar energy for nighttime use, stabilizing grids and enabling 24/7 clean power in India's cities.
learn.c1_text: Lithium-ion batteries store excess solar energy during the day and release it at night. A 10 kWh battery can power a home for 12 hours.
learn.c2_text: Grid-scale batteries (100+ MWh) smooth solar/wind fluctuations. India's largest 200 MWh battery in Tamil Nadu reduced grid instability by 30%.
learn.c3_text: India's battery storage capacity grew 300% in 2023. The PLI scheme aims to manufacture 50 GWh domestically by 2025.
learn.stat1: A 10 kWh battery powers a home for 12 hours
learn.stat2: 200 MWh battery in Tamil Nadu reduced instability by 30%
learn.stat4: PLI scheme targets 50 GWh domestic manufacturing by 2025

learn.title: EV Charging Infrastructure
learn.intro: EV charging networks are essential for India's electric mobility revolution, with different levels meeting diverse needs from homes to highways.
learn.c1_text: Level 1 uses standard 120V outlets, adding 3-5 miles of range per hour. Ideal for overnight home charging but too slow for daily commutes.
learn.c2_text: Level 2 (240V) adds 25 miles/hour, while DC fast charging (480V+) delivers 60-200 miles in 15 minutes. India's highways now have 1,200+ DC fast chargers.
learn.c3_text: India's FAME II policy targets 2.5M EVs by 2023. The BharatCharging@2030 initiative plans 2.5 lakh charging stations nationwide.
learn.stat2: DC fast charging adds 60-200 miles in 15 minutes
learn.stat3: India's FAME II targets 2.5M EVs by 2023
